data.title: Schwab Earnings Preview: Cheap On A PEG Basis, Can The Stock Break Out Of This 3-Year Consolidation?
data.description:
Schwab (NYSE:SCHW) will report their calendar Q2 ’25 financial results on Friday morning, July 18th.

Analyst consensus is expecting $1.10 in earnings per share on $5.73 billion in revenue for expected y-o-y growth of 51% and 22%. Pre-tax income is expected at $2.77 billion for expected y-o-y growth of 44%.

Since Schwab peaked at $96 per share in early February ’22, the stock originally fell to the $50 area (and even a little lower than that) in 2023 (twice) but has slowly made its way back to within 9-iron distance of its former all-time high in the last two years.

Schwab is up 24% YTD (as of Wednesday night’s 7/16 close).

When SCHW peaked in early '22, it was trading at 40x earnings, for what was expected to be 20% y-o-y EPS growth, while today SCHW is trading at 20x for an expected EPS growth rate in ’25 of 37% on revenue growth of 15%.

A high-multiple momentum stock is now a reasonably-valued PEG (pe-to-growth) play.

SCHW EPS and revenue estimate revisions:

SCHW’s 2025 EPS estimated bottomed in late ’24 and has been revised steadily higher since that time.

Schwab’s revenue estimate revisions have followed the same pattern.

In Q1 ’25, revenue grew 18%, pre-tax income was up 33%, and EPS rose 41%. Net new assets alone grew $39 billion in March ’25. Jefferies made an interesting point in their post-earnings analysis that TD Ameritrade’s business is slowly returning to Schwab, accounting for 2%–3% organic growth in Q1 ’25. Jefferies also noted that Schwab currently has roughly 30% wallet share of TDAM’s clients that were onboarded, while that should be closer to 50%.

Summary/conclusion:

With Schwab’s merger/acquisition of TD Ameritrade now behind it, Schwab’s investment thesis returns to a “fundamental” one. Trading at 20x forward EPS for expected earnings growth in ’25, ’26, and ’27 of 37%, 18%, and 16%, on expected revenue growth for the same three years of 15%, 12%, and 8%, the valuation story is self-explanatory.

Technically, a break and heavy volume close above $96–$97, after a three-year consolidation period, would be a very favorable technical sign.

Schwab recently raised the quarterly dividend from $0.25 to $0.27 per share, the first increase in 8 quarters.

As an advisor who has all clients' assets with Charles Schwab as a 3rd-party custodian, I was relieved Schwab didn’t chase the crypto or bitcoin craze in the 2020’s, but watching the rise of Robinhood of late, and in particular after Amazon (AMZN, AMZN:CA) and Walmart (WMT, WMT:CA) announced they were considering converting to stablecoin (US dollar-backed crypto) to reduce the cost of the interchange fees from credit cards, I wondered if this wasn’t the ultimate legitimization of crypto, by America’s two largest retailers, and if Schwab is now behind the “Bitcoin” boat.

As the low-cost leader in the former “discount-broker” realm, Schwab is still an asset-gathering juggernaut. The net new assets that get brought in every quarter is still quite formidable.

With current growth expectations of 37% in EPS and 15% revenue growth in calendar ’25 and the current multiple 20x, the stock is way too cheap here. If a $125 fair value price is used for Schwab, the stock is still just trading at 28x for 37% expected EPS growth this year.

Schwab is this blog’s 2nd largest financial weighting behind JPM (JPM) and in the top 10 of client holdings. Part articles on Schwab are here, and here.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. None of the information above may be updated, and if updated may not be done so in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Freshpet: Stock Plunge And Growth Make For A Dilemma
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Freshpet, Inc. (NASDAQ:FRPT) a Hold rating. I think that it is advisable to be prudent and wait until the company becomes a profitable firm. Since 2016, the company has only achieved positive net earnings in 2024. As such, it has financed its operations by contracting debt and issuing common shares, which is not a sustainable long-term strategy. For this reason, the market has not reacted positively. In fact, Freshpet's stock price plunged by 49.14% in the last year.

From a growth perspective, the company has achieved excellent financial results, with its revenues increasing at a 28.68% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) between 2016 and 2024. In addition, it projects an 84.58% growth in its revenues from 2024 to 2027. In the upcoming years, Freshpet also anticipates increasing its product lines from 14 to 24. This significant growth has been possible due to the considerable expenses that Freshpet has allocated to selling, general, and administrative (SG&A). To grant context, in Q1 2025, the SG&A expenses accounted for 43.79% of the company's revenues.

In 2024, the company held approximately 2.20% of the U.S. pet food market. Consequently, it will need to invest significant financial resources to become a larger player in the industry. For Freshpet, this challenge is positive and negative. On the one hand, the company has room to increase its revenues by 5–15 times in the upcoming years. On the other hand, the company could fall short and end in financial distress, since firms like Nestle have more financial resources to compete with Freshpet. When pondering all the positive and negative aspects of Freshpet, I think it is time to be patient and wait until the company demonstrates that it can generate profit.

The Company

Freshpet is a dog and cat food company headquartered in Bedminster, New Jersey, U.S. The company has one Segment, which consists of the manufacturing, marketing, and distribution of fresh dog and cat food, as well as dog treats, as depicted in the following image.

Its products are intended to be fresh and organic with minimal processing, which provides healthier food for cats and dogs. Since 89% of American families with dogs view their dogs as family members, they are more willing to spend more resources on their pets by buying refrigerated pet food, which is a premium product.

Overview

Growth is the first aspect of Freshpet, as depicted in the following chart. In 2016, its net sales were $129.71 million, and it reached $975.18 million in 2024, for a CAGR of 28.68%. This growth indicates that the company aims to gain market share. For the future, Freshpet will continue to focus on growing. In fact, it expects to reach net sales of $1,800 million in 2027, representing an 84.58% increase compared to 2024.

Investment and Growth are generally interconnected. Companies need to invest financial resources to expand their operations. Turning to Freshpet, the company has three production facilities, namely Freshpet Kitchens Bethlehem, Freshpet Kitchens South, and Freshpet Kitchens Ennis, located in Pennsylvania and Texas. These facilities have 14 production lines, and they are expected to reach more than 24 production lines in the upcoming years. The company said that it can reach $3,000 million in net sales by 2029/2030.

However, the company has an Achilles' heel. When excluding 2024, it has not reported positive net income or positive operating income since at least 2016. In the first quarter of this year, it reported a net loss of $12.70 million and an accumulated deficit of $294.50 million.

To finance its investment in capital expenditures and operations, the company has issued common shares. To provide context, Freshpet had 35,643,964 common shares in 2018, increasing to 48,777,091 common shares in Q1 2025. When there are more common shares, shareholders experience dilution in their ownership stake, as there are more shares in circulation. In addition, the company has relied on debt to finance and expand its operations. In Q1 2025, it had liabilities of $393.07 million that were related to its long-term debt.

Breaking Down Costs

To start with, Freshpet has reported considerable gross profit margins. In 2024 and Q1 2025, its gross profit margin fluctuates around 40%, as depicted in the first chart below. Consequently, the company does not report excessive costs of goods sold. However, its operating profit margin reveals that Freshpet has significant operating expenses. In fact, the company has only reported positive operating profit in 2024, as depicted in the second chart below.

So far, I have not included other companies in the analysis. The following table shows the gross profit margin of Freshpet, Nestlé S.A. (OTCPK:NSRGY), The J. M. Smucker Company (SJM), General Mills, Inc. (GIS), Chewy, Inc. (CHWY), Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL), and Post Holdings, Inc. (POST). These companies are among the leading pet food producers. The table indicates that Freshpet has a higher gross profit margin than the median of its peers. However, it has the second-lowest EBIT and EBITDA margins. Therefore, this analysis reinforces the argument that the company has high operating expenses.

SG&A expenses explain why Freshpet is unprofitable. In Q1 2025, SG&A expenses were $115.29 million, representing 43.79% of the revenues. During that period, the costs of goods sold were $159.46 million. I include the previous figure to emphasize that SG&A expenses are slightly lower than the costs of goods sold.

Selling, General, and Administrative Expenses

Since SG&A expenses are composed of several components, I think it is convenient to analyze these costs. In SG&A expenses, media costs and share-based compensation expenses have increased significantly. By media costs, Freshpet includes costs related to advertising through media outlets. To provide context, between 2022 and Q1 2025 TTM, media costs increased by 90.32%, while share-based compensation expenses grew by 122.82%, as depicted in the following chart. In that period, revenues only gained 70.42%. Media costs and share-based compensation expenses accounted for 16.89% of revenues in Q1 2025 TTM. If the company had cut these expenses by half, it would have ended the period with a positive operating profit.

Advertising in the media is the mechanism that has fueled Freshpet's revenue growth. The investment in media that Freshpet has expended is correlated to its household penetration. This correlation reaches 98%. Therefore, as the company invests more resources in media costs, its revenues increase.

It is evident that the company aims to increase its market share. However, it must reduce its SG&A expenses as a proportion of its net sales to become profitable. Pursuing only growth is not enough. I am of the opinion that Freshpet needs to generate positive earnings to become a compelling investment opportunity. So far, the company has burned financial resources.

Market Size

In 2024, Freshpet reported revenues of $975.18 million. From this total, 98% of its revenues were generated in the U.S. That year, the U.S. pet food market size was $43,450 million. Moreover, Freshpet accounted for only 2.20% of the U.S. pet food market in 2024. It will take years for Freshpet to become a leading company in the pet food industry.

I think the strategy of gaining more market share is correct. With a small market share in the pet food industry, Freshpet will struggle to compete with its larger competitors. However, the company will have to equilibrate growth and profitability.

Stock Price

The market has not been optimistic about Freshpet. Currently, its stock price is at the levels observed in February 2020, as depicted in the following chart. In fact, Freshpet's stock price has experienced a sharp decline since January of this year.

Freshpet mentions that people are hesitant to get a new dog or cat, or replace their pet when it passes away. According to the company, many Americans are not clear about the U.S. economy. Therefore, they prefer to wait until the current period of economic uncertainty disappears before bringing a new pet into their homes. Freshpet also indicates that consumer sentiment is one of the reasons that has slowed down its revenue growth rate. The consumer sentiment index is near its all-time low. This index measures the consumers' opinions about the economy and their personal finances.

Due to the environment of macroeconomic uncertainty in the U.S., the company downgraded its net sales projection for this year. In the previous guidance, it expected net sales in the range of $1,180 million to $1,210 million. The new guidance estimates revenues between $1,120 million and $1,150 million.

Common Shares and Debt

At Freshpet's current stock price, I think that the market will react negatively if it issues common shares. This year, the company's stock price has declined by 54.13%. For instance, if it intends to raise $100 million in common shares, it will have to issue more than twice the ordinary shares that it would have had to issue a year ago.

However, Freshpet still has significant liquid resources. In Q1 2025, it reported $243.73 million in cash and cash equivalents. Meanwhile, in that period, it had $395.70 million in long-term debt. In addition, in Q1 2025, it reported current assets of $ 405.39 million and total liabilities of $509.54 million. As such, I think the company has significant financial resources to sustain its operations. While it is true that the company has reported net losses between 2016 and 2023, it is also true that these losses are relatively small in comparison to its total revenue. To provide context, in 2022, the company reported $58.00 million in net losses, its worst performance from 2016 to 2024, as depicted in the following chart. At that level, the company would burn its cash and cash equivalents in 4.2 years.

Peer Analysis

This section conducts a peer analysis among Freshpet, NSRGY, SJM, GIS, CHWY, CL, and POST. These companies are relevant pet food producers. The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

In the last year, Freshpet's stock price declined by 49.14%. In addition, in a five-year period, its stock price decreased by 21.60%. Among its peers, it is the company with the worst performance in the financial market, a negative aspect.

The valuation metrics suggest that Freshpet is overvalued compared to its peers. For instance, the analyst consensus projects that the company will have a 79.28 P/E, while the median of this ratio in the peer group is 19.76. The company also has higher EV/Sales, EV/EBITDA, and price-to-cash ratios than the median in the peer group.

As commented on in the Breaking Down Costs section, the company has an excellent gross profit margin compared to its peers. However, its EBIT margin is the second lowest value due to its considerable SG&A expenses. With that being said, the company dominates all the growth metrics. For this year, the analyst consensus expects a 20.20% growth rate in Freshpet's revenues. In addition, the revenues grew at a 31.18% CAGR in a five-year period.

Finally, the company also exhibits low debt metrics compared to its peers. For instance, it has a 41.02% debt-to-equity ratio, the lowest value among its peers. Furthermore, it has a 4.91 current ratio. As such, the company has financial resources to pay its obligations.

Overall, Freshpet has better growth and debt ratios than its peers. However, the valuation ratios suggest that the company is undervalued. In addition, the company has experienced a significant decline in its stock price. Pondering all these aspects, I think Freshpet should improve its profit ratios to be an excellent investment opportunity.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I think that it is advisable to wait until the company reports positive earnings. Freshpet has an excellent growth outlook compared to its peers. Its revenues grew at a 28.68% CAGR between 2016 and 2024. In addition, the revenues are expected to increase by 84.58% from 2024 to 2027. Due to this significant expected expansion, the company plans to increase its product lines to 24, from its current 14 lines.

However, the company has only been profitable in 2024. In the first quarter of 2025, it reported a net loss of $12.70 million. These losses were due to the considerable investment dedicated to expanding its operations. To provide context, in Q1 2025, Freshpet's SG&A expenses represented 43.79% of its revenues. The company dedicates significant resources to advertising.

On the other hand, Freshpet has expanded its operations by issuing shares and contracting debt. This strategy is not sustainable in the long term. I believe the company will become an attractive investment opportunity if it can generate profits. When taking all of these aspects into consideration, I think it is time to wait until the company becomes a profitable firm.

data.title: Lumine Group: The Positive Impact Of Its Smaller Size
data.description:
Introduction

In my first coverage of Lumine Group (TSXV:LMN:CA) (OTCPK:LMGIF) in late 2023, a few months after its IPO, I concluded that I favored Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF) (CSU:CA) due to its track record, diversification, and higher organic growth.

While I rightfully stated that Lumine's returns on invested capital would improve with the WideOrbit acquisition, I underestimated the benefits of its smaller size, allowing it to close highly creative deals at much more attractive prices than Constellation.

Although Constellation's investors can't complain about the performance during that time, Lumine Group has delivered much better returns, driven by a 34% increase in revenues and a 55% increase in adjusted FCF per share during 2024.

Lumine Group In A Nutshell

Lumine Group acquires and manages mission-critical software companies operating in media and communications, following Constellation Software's playbook.

We follow the same hurdle rates. We have the same governance and control framework, and we operate very much like an operating group. So it's tried and tested, and it works well, and we fall in line and we support it in any case.

Source: David Nyland (Lumine Group's CEO), 2025 AGM Q&A.

The company spun out from Constellation in 2023 after its largest acquisition (WideOrbit), and currently has a market cap of $9.7B.

Since its early days as part of Volaris (one of Constellation's operating groups), Lumine has acquired 33 companies, which usually have a #1 or #2 market share in the niche where they operate.

The focus on the media and communications spaces reduces industry diversification, but Lumine seeks geographic diversification and low customer concentration, taking advantage of carve-outs and special situations such as bankruptcies to close deals at favorable prices.

Declining Businesses, But Strong Revenue Growth

Lumine's businesses, like most vertical market software companies (VMS), have clauses in their contracts to raise prices with inflation.

While the company delivered positive organic growth during 2021 amid the post-pandemic high-inflation period, the most recent quarters have shown negative organic growth.

Part of the decline has been caused by the integration and onboarding of certain acquisitions, but it could also be that Lumine's portfolio of companies has higher churn rates than its peers.

During a period where both Constellation Software and Topicus.com (TSXV:TOI:CA) (OTCPK:TOITF) delivered positive or neutral organic growth, Lumine had a -8% organic growth in 2024, and a -4% in Q1 2025.

Nonetheless, the company is delivering revenue growth above 25% (2023 was strongly impacted by the WideOrbit acquisition), from which we can infer that, on top of its ability to deploy all its FCF into acquisitions, Lumine is closing deals at attractive prices.

Recent M&A Activity

During its first year as a publicly traded company, Lumine closed 5 acquisitions, having one of its most M&A active years, especially considering the WideOrbit transaction ($490M).

In 2024, only two deals were closed, but Lumine managed to deploy $145M (80% of its adjusted FCF) at attractive prices, taking advantage of Casa Systems, Inc.'s (CASA) bankruptcy.

The most significant acquisition during 2024 was the Device and Service Management Platform businesses from Nokia (NYSE:NOK), which was initially expected to close for up to €185M (~$200M at the time), but Lumine ended up paying $110M (1.15x revenues).

The other transaction that Lumine closed during 2024 was the opportunistic acquisition of Casa System's cloud native 5G assets for $32M (0.76x revenues) following the Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing.

The 2025 acquisitions (Datafusion Systems and Vidispine) were closed during Q2, so they haven't yet impacted Lumine's financial statements, and prices have not been disclosed.

Vidispine provides management systems to media companies, and while I haven't been able to access its financials, revenues are estimated to be around $5.2M, so I don't expect a significant impact from this acquisition.

Datafusion Systems is a UAE provider of solutions used in border control and territory surveillance.

Price Paid On Acquisitions

Although Lumine hasn't disclosed the exact multiples for last year's acquisitions, we can approximate them by comparing the cash deployed with the increase in FCF.

Adjusted FCF, which I measure as cash from operations excluding change in net working capital, less D&A of tangible assets, increased by $69M in 2024 (from $113M in 2023)

When excluding the increase in share count, we get a $62M increase. In this increase, however, we also need to take into account the late 2023 acquisition of Synchronoss for $38M (0.6x revenues).

Considering the $180M spent on the three acquisitions and the $62M increase in adjusted FCF, we can approximate the price paid to ~3x FCF, in line with the 30.9% adjusted FCF margin delivered during the most recent quarter.

The low valuation multiple paid on these acquisitions may surprise some readers, but it is important to take into account that these are low-growth or even declining businesses, so Lumine needs to squeeze as much FCF as possible while they still deliver it.

Expectations: Organic Growth Will Improve

Lumine's FCF growth will be driven by its ability to deploy capital, the multiple paid on acquisitions (ROIC), and organic growth. Since it generates less than one-tenth of Constellation Software's FCF, it should benefit from its smaller size to find attractive deals.

Last year, the company improved its operating and FCF margins in every quarter (YoY), and delivered its best Q1 in 2025 with an 18.7% operating margin and 30.9% adjusted FCF margin. As intangible assets get depreciated, returns on invested capital will keep their upward trend, converging to a figure around 25% to 30% (in line with WideOrbit's ROIC).

As previously stated, organic growth hasn't performed well in recent quarters, which has been caused by short-term headwinds from the Nokia and Casa System acquisitions:

The negative organic growth during the year ended December 31, 2024 is primarily related to the carve-out acquisitions completed in Q2 2024 which are continuing to go through integration and onboarding of best practices where a reduction in organic growth can be expected as we re-evaluate contracts and projects.

Source: MD&A FY2024.

Nonetheless, the decline in organic growth improved during the last quarter, and given that WideOrbit is delivering positive organic growth, I expect an improvement in the following quarters as the most recent acquisitions are integrated.

Valuation

At the moment, Lumine is generating over $50M in adjusted FCF per quarter, and its shares are trading at 44x its expected 2025 adjusted FCF.

In the model presented below, I forecast a low but positive organic growth after 2027. If the company deploys all its capital into acquisitions, which is a fair assumption considering Lumine's track record, acquisitions should drive above 20% revenue growth.

As Lumine becomes larger, I factor in a decline in the valuation multiple to 34x LTM FCF, which provides a certain margin of safety to our assumptions.

Note: The 2025-2030 period uses a 1.37 USD/CAD exchange rate.

For 2025, given that only two acquisitions have been announced so far, and one of them will likely have a small impact, I assume a lower percentage of capital deployed compared to previous years.

Since the timing of the acquisitions will always be lumpy, I assume that over the next years, all the FCF generated will be deployed into acquisitions.

Based on these assumptions, and including the decrease in valuation, Lumine's stock would deliver a 16.8% CAGR over the next five and a half years.

Risks

Even with a decline in valuation multiples, Lumine could deliver attractive returns to its shareholders over the long term, but there are some risks to take into account.

First, the company trades at the Toronto Venture Exchange, it has a relatively low float, and lower trading volumes and liquidity. While this shouldn't bother Lumine's long-term investors, the stock can present significant volatility and it might be difficult to sell a substantial amount of shares quickly.

Lumine has an average daily trading volume of ~CAD3M, much lower than Constellation Software's 3-month average of ~CAD152M per day.

The other risk I would highlight is running out of acquisitions in the media and communications space. Although the company now has a relatively small size, the focus on a particular niche could limit its ability to deploy capital over the years.

On the positive side of covering a particular niche, Lumine is facing less competition:

In terms of copycats, yes, so yes, we haven't seen yet too many or any real significant communications and media vertical market software consolidators.

And I think it's partially because of how specialized the space is. The barriers to entry from a domain knowledge perspective are pretty high.

Source: David Nyland (Lumine Group's CEO), 2025 AGM Q&A.

Conclusion

Since the first time I reviewed Lumine, the company has demonstrated its ability to close opportunistic acquisitions at attractive prices, and I underestimated the benefits of its smaller size.

In light of the performance of the business, and despite the temporary headwinds in organic growth, I retract the title I gave to that article (Lumine Group: Current Valuation Might Not Be Justified), as I believe Lumine's premium to Constellation Software is justified.

When the most recent acquisitions are integrated, organic growth figures will improve, and returns on invested capital will continue their upward trend.

Lumine's shares could trade sideways for some months to consolidate the recent stock appreciation (unless a significant acquisition is announced), and that is the reason for my hold rating.

Nonetheless, I have a positive view on the business, and I expect it to keep outperforming due to its smaller size and higher chances of finding highly accretive deals with attractive rates of return.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Interest Rate Debate
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Biggest theme this week: increasing tension between Trump and Powell (0:25). Interesting treasury markets (4:20). Airlines earnings, sector signs (5:10). Netflix reporting; valuation concerns (7:30). Stocks continuing overall melt up (8:00).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, welcome to a special Thursday edition of Wall Street Lunch. Thanks for joining us in Kim Khan's absence.

Brian Stewart: Always special to be here.

RS: Indeed. Talk to us about where we're at Thursday afternoon, July 17th.

BS: I think the biggest theme so far this week has been the increasing tension between Trump and Powell. Trump's gotten more belligerent about removing Powell lately. There were signs yesterday that his firing might be imminent.

However, Trump backed off from that. So we're kind of still in the limbo that we've been in for a while with Trump sniping at Powell and Powell maintaining a stony silence about the pressure that he's feeling.

It's a complicated subject. In the macro view, the most important issue is the independence of the Fed. There's a concern that if politicians start either directly fixing interest rates or have enough leverage over the Fed that they have a huge influence on the the current interest rate that will just end up with a zero interest rate in perpetuity.

It's always gonna be in a politician's best interest to have the lowest possible interest rates. That gets you low mortgage rates. It gets you low borrowing rates. So there's always gonna be political pressure to have rates super low.

Trump in fact is pushing for an extremely low rate. He's called on multiple occasions for rates to be cut by three percentage points, so down to the one percent area, which traditionally has been where things end up in dangerous emergency situations.

So it was in the 0% land for a long time after the financial crisis. It got down to 1% after the dot com bubble exploded. So you're talking about a rate that's usually only on the table for special circumstances, and that's what Trump's been pushing for lately.

RS: What else would you say about recent inflation data that we've seen? And anything to note about the bond markets, the dollar, or anything else to add to this part of the conversation?

BS: The most interesting thing about the pressure that's coming lately is it it, in a way, economically, just looking at the data, it seems to be coming out of nowhere, the idea that we would cut rates to 1%.

Again, the political pressure for that makes a lot of sense. It's the same political pressure that keeps the government cutting taxes and raising spending. So it's not that surprising in that frame.

But if you look at the economic news, it's pretty solid, 4.1% unemployment rate. That's the low end of unemployment, that's very healthy levels.

There was a contraction in terms of GDP in the first quarter, but signs so far this quarter show growth in the the mid 2% range, which is healthy where you would want it.

So there doesn't seem to be a real economic crisis bubbling up unless Trump has information that we don't have.

Meanwhile, inflation, which the core inflation rate was 2.9% in the most recent report. That's traditionally at the high end. It's disinflationary when compared to the spike that we saw in the post COVID era.

But if you look historically, 2.9% would be the high end of the range ex COVID for the twenty first century so far. So if we were sitting in 2015 with a 2.9% core CPI rate, that would be dangerously high.

And so you can understand Powell's reluctance to cut rates, especially since I think Powell probably feels burned by the post COVID situation. He left rates low for a longer time than a lot of people wanted him to and inflation got a little bit out of control.

And so I think he's very gun shy about making the same mistake again. I think looking at the the treasury markets is kind of interesting. The thirty year is currently around 5%. That's just off its highs of the year. It was 4% last September. The thirty year rate is higher than it was. It started the year around 4.8%. So it's actually gone up in 2025.

The movement in the lower duration treasuries is less substantial. The ten years around 4.4%, the five year around 4%. Both of those are down from their positions at the beginning of the year.

So you see most of the selling going on at the longer end of the yield curve. So as investors, I would keep an eye on that as this wrangling continues. I think that you're gonna see the signs of displeasure more on the longer end.

RS: Recent earnings, anything to update us on? We saw some reports out of the airlines yesterday.

BS: Yeah. United (UAL) had its earnings last night. It was choppy immediately after the report. The company hit its bottom line, beat on the bottom line, but missed on the top line.

However, the stock at last check was up about 3%. Most of the problems that it faced during the quarter were from flight disruptions in Newark. So I think investors are taking that as a sign that this was a limited situation. It was outside the company's control. And so the underlying operations remain strong.

And this follows up on a report from Delta (DAL) from last week. Delta jumps about 12% following its earnings report, and it beat expectations, set stronger than expected guidance, said that demand trends have stabilized at levels that are about flat to last year.

Meanwhile, corporate demand remains steady. So signs out of the airlines if we're framing this also in terms of the overall economic picture and the debate between Trump and Powell, the signs from the airlines are that demand remains pretty strong for air travel, which is usually a pretty good sign both of the health of the consumer and health of corporate spending.

We're gonna get more information next week. Southwest (LUV) is gonna report its earnings. The (JETS) ETF, which tracks the overall airline industry, it's up 46% since its April low. So you've seen a lot of strength in airlines.

Things got pretty rocky for the airlines earlier this year with worries about the international situation, plus the escalating violence in the Israel Iran conflict, which spiked oil briefly.

So I think that what you're seeing now is those geopolitical concerns are starting to back off a little bit, and you're seeing a relatively healthy economic situation for the airlines.

RS: Netflix (NFLX) is also reporting today after the close.

I wanted to note that we've had two recent episodes on our Investing Experts podcast. Really, really bullish thoughts and analysis on Netflix, but, serious valuation concerns.

A lot of bullishness, but not necessarily long. So if you want a deeper dive into Netflix. Anything to say about Netflix or any other stocks reporting or having recently reported?

BS: I think you summarized it really well. The signs are that Netflix has been solidifying itself as the leader in the streaming space. It's had a rebound lately, but the question becomes whether that rebound is a little overdone.

So yeah, exactly what you said. Valuation is the main concern going to the earnings now.

RS: So you wanna take us home with a brief minute or two on today's markets? And listeners, please note that tomorrow, we will be back with our regularly scheduled Wall Street Roundup. Look for that on Friday afternoon. Take it away, Brian. Thanks for joining us again.

BS: Yeah. Sure. Stocks are up mildly today, kinda just continuing the overall melt up that we've seen generally.

Major news today is the retail sales report and jobless claims. Those came in relatively benign.

So again, you're seeing a solid to strong economic situation, and I think that the debate over interest rates are going to be the the main headline getter for the rest of the month until the Fed announces its next move at the end of the month.

data.title: This Ain't Your Grandpa's GE: Aerospace Engines Firing On All Cylinders
data.description:
Introduction

Earnings season is here!

Although I believe that many people overhype earnings season, it's still a valuable time to get updates on the companies in your portfolio or watchlist.

And, even more importantly, by listening to what the most influential executives have to say, we can learn a lot about the economy, major trends, and industries.

Today, I'm writing a special article on GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE), my fourth-largest investment in a portfolio of just 16 ultra-high-conviction investments.

I have brought the company up many times in multi-ticker articles since I started buying the aerospace giant last year, as I consider it one of the best companies to benefit from multi-decade secular growth in global aerospace and defense.

As I wrote in an article on June 22, the global population is expected to add 1.2 billion people by 2044. The global middle class, meaning people with the funds to pay for flights, is expected to grow even faster. Moreover, over the next two decades, Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) expects more than 43,000 new aircraft deliveries, most of which will be fleet additions.

Essentially, by 2040, close to 50,000 aircraft will need engines. That's twice as many as in 2024, with nearly half of these being older planes that come with substantial maintenance opportunities. This does not include military demand.

GE Aerospace is, by far, the biggest leader, as it powers roughly 75% of all commercial planes on the planet through its own engines and its Joint Venture with Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF), which produced the CFM56, the most successful engine in the history of global aviation.

Even better, roughly 70% of its revenues come from aftermarket operations and services, which shows just how well GE benefits from aging engines and rapid demand from new launches.

It also generates revenue from defense programs, which comes with demand from platforms like the F-16 and an installed base of nearly 30,000 engines.

In this article, I'll use the opportunity to provide a full overview of my thesis, assess its latest Q2 earnings, and tell you what I make of the current risk/reward.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

GE Is Firing On All Cylinders

GE, which currently has close to 50,000 engines in service, has reported blowout numbers, not only confirming my thesis but exceeding all expectations, supported by 27% more orders, 23% higher revenue, 23% higher operating profit, and a 38% surge in adjusted earnings per share.

Meanwhile, free cash flow almost doubled.

Allow me to throw some more numbers at you, as its commercial business saw 28% higher orders, 29% higher service revenue, and a 35% boost in equipment revenue. New orders include:

  • 40 GE9X engines for wide-body aircraft from Qatar Airways.
  • Engines for 32 Boeing 787 aircraft ordered by International Airlines Group. These will be powered by GEnx engines with an 85% life-of-program win rate.
  • The U.S. Air Force awarded the company a $5 billion contract to develop the F110 engines.

Note that defense programs are a major supporter of R&D, which explains why the company has such a wide business moat, as it has incredible R&D power, with a large part of R&D funded by defense programs.

Meanwhile, in defense, orders rose by 24% with a book-to-bill ratio of 1.2x, indicating that for every dollar in finished work, the giant gets $1.20 in new orders, an indication of accelerating revenue growth.

These numbers are supported by favorable industry fundamentals.

For example, in Q2 '25, global flight departures were up almost 4%. On a full-year basis, GE expects low-single-digit growth in departures, with some uncertainty in the second half of this year.

On a long-term basis, the company expects the market to grow at a mid-single-digit CAGR through at least 2028, driven by strong commercial demand, as air traffic growth is expected to beat global GDP growth. Asian nations and the Middle East are expected to be positive outliers.

In defense, global modernization and localization trends, combined with funding for sixth-generation aircraft programs (>$1 billion domestically), are supporting growth expectations, especially internationally, due to rising geopolitical tensions.

That's one of the reasons I'm "very long" in defense.

So far, so good.

As much as I like these numbers, the best is yet to come, which is the company's outlook and measures to remain on top of its game.

An Even Brighter Future

For 2025, the company raised its guidance, expecting mid-teens revenue growth (up from low-double digits), with CES (its commercial business) growing in the high teens and commercial equipment growth up to 20%.

It now sees no less than $5.60 in adjusted EPS and a lower bound of $6.5 billion in free cash flow. It also raised the higher bounds of these guidance ranges and sees up to high-single-digit revenue growth in defense operations.

The company even provided long-term guidance.

By 2028, GE Aerospace expects double-digit annual revenue growth, operating profit of $11.5 billion (up $3 billion from 2025), and free cash flow of $8.5 billion, which would be a $1.5 billion increase from prior projections.

Moreover, adjusted EPS is expected to reach $8.40, with margins expanding to more than 21%. According to the company, and unsurprisingly given the industry fundamentals we discussed, this outlook reflects strong commercial services performance, operational leverage, and a growing installed base.

Essentially, these comments are supported by the chart below:

  • Commercial/defense demand is expected to substantially boost the volume/mix component.
  • Operational leverage is expected to achieve an almost similar boost from productivity gains.
  • The growing installed base supports longer-term growth.
  • Pricing is the smallest component. While it's significant, the company is still seeing huge secular tailwinds without being reliant on price gains for operating income growth.

With regard to longer-term growth, the company isn't sitting still when it comes to paving the road for elevated shareholder returns.

A Lot More To Be Upbeat About

A big part of the company's success is CEO Larry Culp's focus on the FLIGHT DECK program. The veteran CEO has a long history, including implementing the Danaher Business System at Danaher (DHR).

FLIGHT DECK is GE Aerospace's equivalent and the tool that has guided the company through major spinoffs. Now, it aims to further streamline operations, including its massive maintenance businesses and complex supply chain.

During its Q2 '25 call, the company noted it is addressing supply chain constraints through a dedicated technology and operations team, improving material input by 10% compared to Q1 25, and supplier delivery reliability to more than 95%.

It also plans to invest north of $1 billion in U.S. factories and supply chain infrastructure. That's on top of $1 billion in MRO and component facilities over the next five years, boosting capacity by 40% through 2030. Major projects include tripling the installed base of its LEAP engine program and doubling its GEnx base, two of the engines that form the backbone of its current portfolio.

In order to remain on top, the company is investing up to 8% of its revenue in R&D, focusing on next-gen programs like its CFM RISE program, which has a 20% improvement in fuel burn by focusing on very unusual propulsion, as it's essentially an open rotor engine.

Moreover, GE Aerospace is a pioneer in composite fan blades, which were introduced on the GE90 and are now used in GEnx, LEAP, and GE9X engines.

I am bringing this up because these lighter, stronger blades improve durability and fuel efficiency, with the GE9X coming with fourth-generation blades. The CFM RISE program will incorporate these advanced composite blades, essentially leveraging 30 years of experience to improve durability and efficiency, one of the benefits that comes with its huge business moat.

In the defense space, the company is making tremendous progress with programs like the XA102 adaptive cycle engine for the Next-Generation Adaptive Propulsion program. This program is supported by $750 million in funding. Besides that, the company is investing in hypersonics and working with the Global Combat Air Program with the United Kingdom and Japan, essentially winning bids in all major global (allied) defense programs.

Furthermore, roughly 40% of CFM56 engines have never had a major shop visit, with 3-4% annual retirements by 2026. This, too, is expected to fuel growth. The same goes for 70% of its GE90 fleet.

Where's The Shareholder Value

The company's success has not gone unnoticed by the market.

Over the past three years, GE shares have appreciated almost 600%, beating the 70% return of the market by a margin that can be seen from space.

This has also pushed its dividend yield down to just 0.5%.

Needless to say, GE is the wrong choice for income-focused investors.

It is, however, still a good choice for total return (dividends + capital gains) investors, as it uses its financial success to improve shareholder value through at least three key points.

  • Investment in growth: The company prioritizes investments in current and future programs, including the multi-billion-dollar growth programs we just discussed.
  • Shareholder returns: GE Aerospace plans to return more than 100% of free cash flow to shareholders through 2026, increasing its commitment to $24 billion (up from $19 billion) via $19 billion in share buybacks and $5 billion in dividends (30% of its total net income). After 2026, at least 70% of free cash flow will be returned every year. Its most recent dividend hike was 29% on February 14, which was a nice Valentine's Day present.
  • Mergers and acquisitions: The company is open to opportunistic bolt-on acquisitions with a high threshold for strategic and financial fit, meaning it won't just spend dollars on M&A for the sake of buying "random" innovation. That makes sense, as it has the core capabilities to compete without being forced into risky M&A deals.

Now comes the bad news.

After its earnings release, the stock weakened roughly 2%, which is not a sign that the market distrusts GE's outlook but a sign that a lot of good news has been priced in.

The company, which enjoys an A- credit rating, trades at a blended P/E ratio of 51x, which makes it the most expensive stock in my entire portfolio. Even if we consider that analysts see close to 20% annual EPS growth, it's not a valuation that screams "buy me."

However, I'm not giving the stock a Hold rating, as GE is a Buy on weakness for me. I am consistently monitoring the stock, ready to expand my position on market corrections.

Its growth rates are simply too high for me to go with a Hold rating, as I still expect GE shares to beat the S&P 500 (SP500) over the next 5-10 years.

Takeaway

GE Aerospace's latest results prove why it's one of my highest-conviction investments.

With elevated growth in orders, revenue, and free cash flow, in addition to a $5 billion defense contract and a dominant 75% share in commercial aviation engines, this company is firing on all cylinders, confirming my long-term investment thesis.

However, what excites me most are the long-term growth opportunities.

By 2028, GE expects $8.5 billion in free cash flow, double-digit annual revenue growth, and an 85% win rate on key engine programs.

When adding $24 billion in shareholder returns by 2026 and groundbreaking R&D like the CFM RISE engine, my thesis only gets stronger.

While its equity isn't cheap, GE is a buy-on-weakness play for me.

Risks To My Thesis

Risks include supply chain disruptions, delays in next-gen engine programs, or a downturn in air travel demand, especially if economic growth slows.

Meanwhile, defense budget cuts could hurt its military operations.

At more than 50x earnings, the margin of error is slim.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pagaya Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Introduction

Back in December 2024, I highlighted Pagaya Technologies Ltd. (NASDAQ:PGY) as TQI's top pick for 2025, labeling it a "Generational Buy" at $9.74 per share:

  • Artificial intelligence is driving US equity markets, with application companies like Pagaya poised to benefit significantly, similar to the Internet revolution.
  • Despite credit losses and geopolitical tensions, Pagaya is progressing towards GAAP profitability, with strong partnerships and market expansion set to fuel future growth.
  • Pagaya is an absolute and relative bargain, presenting a compelling long-term investment opportunity compared to its peers.
  • Considering Pagaya's massive business tailwinds, huge growth potential, and attractive valuation, PGY is our top stock idea for 2025.

Source: Pagaya Technologies: My Top Stock Pick For 2025.

And, since then, Pagaya's stock has rallied by more than 200%:

Now, with broad stock market indices sitting at all-time highs and retail investor enthusiasm through the roof, skeptics and bears will likely deem Pagaya's rapid stock rise as a "FOMO" and "meme mania" rally. However, I firmly believe we are witnessing a fundamentals-driven re-rating in PGY stock, one that we saw coming well before it started:

From an absolute and relative valuation standpoint, Pagaya is an absolute bargain. Interestingly, Mr. Market is rewarding Upstart Holdings, Inc. (UPST) [Pagaya's peer] for its secular growth opportunity in AI-lending and macro tailwinds heading into 2025 while, at the same time, trading Pagaya at dirt-cheap levels. In my view, Pagaya is the better business, and PGY deserves to be valued higher than UPST.

If Pagaya delivers on GAAP profitability in 2025, Mr. Market will likely be forced to re-rate its stock. And given Upstart's current pricing, Pagaya could go up ~10x from current levels. Given the current setup, I believe Pagaya is the most asymmetric equity investment opportunity heading into the new year.

Fundamentals Are Justifying The Re-Rating

On 17th July 2025, Pagaya announced robust preliminary results for Q2 2025, with all key financial metrics (network volume, revenue, and GAAP net income) now projected to exceed management guidance and consensus Street estimates handily:

While the final Q2 results will be posted on 7th August 2025, Pagaya's Q2 revenue figure of $326M [+30.4% y/y] marks a significant acceleration in the business, handily beating the consensus analyst estimate of $299.24M.

Furthermore, Pagaya's GAAP Net Income is now expected to land at +$17M for Q2 2025, well above management's guidance range of $0 to $10M.

Now, as I have said in the past, achieving GAAP profitability is a significant milestone for the company, as this feat effectively validates Pagaya's business model. Hence, today's 20%+ jump in PGY stock is no surprise!

The Stock Has Room To Run

Given that Pagaya's annualized network volume of $10.4B is still only a tiny sliver of the $4T+ US consumer loan market [$850B SAM for Personal, Auto, and POS], in my mind, Pagaya is primed to deliver profitable growth for several years to come, potentially decades.

Over the medium term [next 3-5 years], Pagaya aims to scale network volumes from ~$10B to ~$25B. If we assume no improvements in Pagaya's take rates [despite increasing value delivered to partners from an AI network that logically gets better with scale and time], the revenue base should also grow from ~$1B in 2024 to ~$2.5B over the next 3-5 years, at a CAGR rate of 20-35%. To implement a margin of safety, let's assume a 25% CAGR sales growth for Pagaya over the next five years. Please note this growth assumption is only slightly higher than the forecasted growth rate of ~23.5% CAGR from 2023-2033 for the Global AI in Lending Market, as seen in the introduction section of this report.

On margins, Pagaya currently has an FRLPC of 4.3% of network volume, with its most mature offering - Personal Loan - generating an FRLPC of 6.6% of network volume. Over time, both Auto and POS products are expected to mirror the margins of the Personal Loan product as they mature and scale up. Considering Pagaya's current operational performance and potential operating leverage from increased scale, I see no reason why Pagaya cannot deliver 2-3% of network volumes [20-30% of revenues (take rate of 10%)] in free cash flows. Again, let's go with the lower end of this range in our valuation model to implement a margin of safety.

Source: Pagaya Technologies: My Top Stock Pick For 2025.

For the model base, I have updated the revenue assumption to $1.25B for today's valuation exercise. All other model assumptions are relatively straightforward, but if you have any questions, feel free to share them in the comments section below.

Here's our updated valuation model for Pagaya:

In light of Pagaya's rally from the single digits into the high twenties, PGY's long-term risk/reward has naturally deteriorated; however, it is still quite lucrative, with PGY's 5-year price target rising to $148.62 per share, implying a 5-year expected CAGR growth rate of ~39%.

Concluding Thoughts

Credit is the bedrock of the American economy. While macroeconomic and geopolitical risks continue to hang in the background, Pagaya's two-sided, B2B AI network will continue to transform consumer lending, one transaction at a time. The business is now GAAP profitable, validating the ongoing re-rating in PGY stock. Despite already 3x'ing this year, I see a lot of room for further upside from here, with my fair value estimate for PGY sitting at $102 per share [roughly ~3.5x from current levels] and the 5-year expected CAGR of ~39% sitting well above our investment hurdle rate.

Key Takeaway: Pagaya remains a Strong Buy in the $20s.

Thanks for reading, and happy investing. Please share your thoughts, questions, or concerns in the comments section below.

data.title: Asset Allocation Insights - Quarterly Report: June 2025
data.description:
By Indrani De, CFA, PRM, Head of Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell | Zhaoyi Yang, CFA, FRM, Sr Manager, Global Investment Research | Henry Morrison-Jones, CFA, Manager, Global Investment Research

Macro decoupling increases diversification opportunities amid sustained volatility. Increased role for Fixed Income, Alternatives and International (ex US) Equities.

Macro backdrop conducive to high volatility and dispersion

Global growth faces headwinds (IMF) due to continued high uncertainty and tariff impacts. Inflation vs. growth concerns differ between the US and other DMs, leading to divergent central bank paths.

Equity market leadership shifts: Developed Europe shines, while the US underperforms

Dev Europe ex UK equities led performance YTD. The UK & China also outperformed the FTSE All-World, while the US lagged. The change in leadership is underpinned by valuations that have compressed in the US but expanded in other markets. US valuations remain more expensive than most markets, while earnings growth is expected to lag, suggesting the US could continue to underperform.

Greater role for fixed income in multi-asset portfolios

Yields backed up to pre-GFC era, curves steepened due to uncertainty, fiscal expansion & higher term premium; positive for ALM & income investors. YTD returns in FI (~6% for rates, ~5% for credit) match that of equity (~6%). Lower stock-bond correlations have brought back the diversification benefits of FI.

Alternatives: Listed infrastructure and commodities (mainly gold) highly accretive in portfolios

Listed infrastructure delivered equity-like total returns, high income yield and low correlation to equities. Gold shone on the back of high uncertainty and structural geopolitical risk led demand.

Tailwinds for emerging markets (EM)

EM expected to outgrow DM at an increasing rate post-2026 (IMF). Over 3M, EM equities (6.0%) outperformed DMs (2.6%). EM 7-10yr govt bond spreads vs US have tightened since 2022 (sign of resilience), while their equity valuations relative to macro growth lag those of DMs (scope for re-rating).

The US dollar no longer the safe haven; currency hedging became more important

In an unusual pattern, Euro, Yen and many currencies strengthened vs the US dollar in a stress environment. Bigger role of currency returns raise the importance of hedging decisions.

Macro decoupling leads to increased diversification opportunities

Decoupling in equities led by US diverging from international markets (true for the UK & Developed APAC with a smaller margin). EM equity correlation with most geographic areas (in equity & fixed income) fell to <0.5. Commodity correlation to stocks and bonds turned more negative. Correlations within FI decreased, but to a lesser extent than in equities.

Chart 1: US uncertainty surged to a decade high, in response to changing goods tariffs. Equity volatility picked up more in the US than the rest of the world, but spillover effects are obvious as trade policies matter to partners.

Chart 2: Neither the weakened US dollar nor bearish Treasuries have proved reliable safe havens on tariff shock, unlike during GFC or Covid. The double whammy of US debt downgrade and tariffs may have led to this unusual case.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG and US Federal Reserve. All data as of May 31, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Financial Markets Overview

The announcement of tariffs on April 2nd sent shock waves through financial markets. Subsequent de-escalation of trade tensions in May contributed to a broad "risk-on" mood in recent weeks. However, policy uncertainty and the resultant downside risks remain high, creating a backdrop that is conducive to high volatility.

Divergent inflation expectations lead to divergent monetary policy between the US and the rest of the world. While global growth is slowing and tariffs raise inflation concerns, the trade-off between growth and inflation concerns differs across the world. US, as the epicenter of higher tariffs with every trading partner, faces both inflation and growth concerns, while rest of the world worries more about slower growth. Macro divergence lays the ground for higher dispersion across geographic markets.

Higher risk from deteriorating fiscal positions and uncertainty-led increases in term premium is getting priced into DM sovereigns, particularly so in US Treasuries. Steepening yield curves are now mostly a global phenomenon.

Credit spreads gyrated with tariff moves, with some spread widening as investors demand higher risk premia over rising inflation and policy uncertainties. However, credit markets responded to tariff shock in a restrained way (compared to Covid & post-Covid inflation shocks), as liquidity conditions remain benign. Euro bonds outperformed the US & EM broadly (in USD terms), with a stronger Euro leading to currency benefits. Euro bonds still trade cheaper than their US & EM equivalents.

US equity underperformance in 2025, a significant reversal of its role as the best performing equity markets in the GFC-2024 era, has largely been driven by compression in valuation. Still high current P/Es, real yields and other macro indicators suggest this trend could continue. Meanwhile higher growth estimates in other markets with lower valuations vs the US, make a case for continued convergence in cross-regional valuations, but sentiments indicate risks remain in Europe.

While policy uncertainty puts downward pressure on growth globally, EM growth is still expected to be 2.6x that of DM. Within equities, EM outperformed DM over 3M, but lagged in fixed income (largely due to the China effect). There is wide dispersion within EM equities, reflecting their relative exposure to US tariffs(dependent on whether they run trade deficits or surplus with the US).

Commodities exhibited high dispersion, as slowing growth and rising uncertainty favored gold, while growth concerns dampened oil & copper. Structural de-dollarization also favors gold, and AI and green transition demand offsets some cyclical weakness for copper. The relationship of gold vs oil today is a sharp reversal from Sept 2023. Over 1Y, gold returned 40%+ vs 10%+ for most equities & alternatives.

Listed infrastructure & real estate had healthy returns, just behind that of equities in last 12M. They are less correlated to equities than to fixed income. Listed infrastructure had a low correlation to equities over 12M. High return and low correlation to equities highlight the portfolio benefits of listed infrastructure.

The US dollar did not exhibit its usual safe-haven status in the stress period following wide-ranging US policy uncertainty since the start of 2025. The recent US credit rating downgrade was another headwind for the US dollar. The high share of currency return in G20 equity total returns in USD in the last 3M highlights the growing role of portfolio hedging decisions.

Capital flows are reflecting the changing and uncertain environment. 3M investor flows diverge sharply from 12M patterns as investors turn towards defensive equity sectors, commodities and alternatives.

In 1Y vs 3Y, the return gap between equities and fixed income narrowed, and volatility for both asset classes broadly drifted lower, despite higher uncertainties, thus improving the return/risk ratios. This implies a growing role for FI in multi-asset portfolios.

The last 12 months have boosted diversification opportunities. Declining stock-bond correlation helps fixed income again provide diversification to equities. 1Y vs 3Y correlation differences show a broad-based geographic decoupling, with significant correlation declines in most asset classes. The decoupling is stronger in equities than in fixed income, largely driven by US equities which diverged from international markets. UK and Developed APAC equities also decoupled from other markets, but by a narrower margin. EM equity correlations mostly dropped by ~0.2-0.5 and had <0.5 correlation with most geographic areas, in both equity and fixed income. Commodities turned more negatively correlated to both stocks and bonds. Correlations within fixed income also decreased broadly, though to a lesser extent than for equities. The exceptions were: Treasuries become more correlated to inflation-linked bonds and real estate’s correlation with World high yield credit rose to 0.93.

Chart 1: Sovereigns and IG corporates outperformed equities over 3M (in USD), resulting in fixed income delivering returns that were comparable to equities YTD. Within equities, Value significantly outperformed Growth YTD. Commodities posted losses over 3M, alongside the US dollar, trimming 12M gains. Over 12M, global equities led gains across asset classes, with Global Core Infrastructure and Listed Real Estate not far behind, then followed by World HY.

Macroeconomic Backdrop

De-escalation of trade tensions in May contributed to a broad "risk-on" mood in recent weeks, however policy uncertainty and the resultant downside risks remain high.

The IMF reduced 2025 growth forecasts across all major markets in April (Chart 1). Soft and hard data series have begun to diverge in the US, with business and consumer-related survey data weakening to begin the year but both the retail sales and private capital investment components of GDP have remained strong (Charts 2 & 3). US hard data may still be strong given Q1 distortions to front-run tariff impacts, but the expected convergence of soft & hard data increases the potential for continued financial market volatility.

Q1 GDP growth was negative in the US but broadly positive for most other developed markets. Net exports were a major negative contributor to US GDP growth in Q1 but a major positive contributor to other markets.

IMF forecasts indicate that EMs are expected to grow at a quicker rate than DMs in the coming years (Chart 4). Forecasts also show that while US growth is expected to moderate in 2025 and 2026, other developed markets are expected to see some recovery in growth in 2026 and beyond (Chart 5). US GDP growth in 2026 is now forecast to be below economic potential, indicating significant economic slack.

Chart 1: The IMF lowered its 2025 GDP growth forecasts across all major markets in April on the back of heightened trade policy uncertainty, suggesting that the impact of tariffs would be broad based.

Chart 2: The University of Michigan consumer sentiment survey reached its lowest point since 2022 in April 2025. Despite this, y/y US retail sales growth has continued to rise with tariff impacts yet to be felt.

Chart 3: US Manufacturing PMI has weakened YTD, falling to 48.5 in May. However, the private domestic fixed investment component of GDP growth has yet to see significant softening, instead growing +1.9% q/q in Q1.

Chart 4: The IMF predicts that emerging markets will outgrow developed markets at an increasing rate post-2026, highlighting their growing importance in the global economy.

Chart 5: Looking out to 2026, the IMF expects growth to pick up in the UK and Euro Zone but moderate in the US and China. Other developed markets are expected to catch up with the US as growth returns to potential.
Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of May 31, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Divergent inflation expectations leads to divergent monetary policy between the US and the rest of the world.

While inflation has made significant progress over the past two years (Chart 1), concerns remain, especially in the US. For most DMs, 1-3 year inflation breakevens is now below 2%, while for the US, breakevens have continued to rise over the last year (Chart 2). Rising inflation expectations could restrict monetary easing in the US. Since the US election, the US has already seen one of the sharpest reversals in forecasts for rate cuts in 2025 (Chart 3). The US is now an outlier among DMs.

Historically, while equity returns in developed markets have largely tracked GDP expansion over the past decade, some EMs have seen equity performance lag what economic fundamentals might suggest (Chart 5). If using the relationship between GDP growth and equity performance as a barometer, emerging market growth forecasts indicate the potential for a re-rating or catch up in equity performance looking forward. For developed market economies, IMF forecasts of a narrowing growth gap between the US and non-US developed markets suggest that differences in equity performance between the US and the rest of the world should also narrow.

Chart 1: Although inflation across most DM economies has made significant progress, it remains stubbornly above central bank targets for many markets, demanding caution from central banks.

Chart 2: In the US, short term inflation expectations have increased markedly, adding additional complexity to central bank decision-making. In Europe, short term inflation expectations are lower than in the US.

Chart 3: Forecasts for end-2025 policy rates across developed economies show that since the US election, the US has seen the sharpest reversal in expectations for policy rate cuts among its peers.

Chart 4: Policy uncertainty remains high, yet recent risk-on momentum has led equities to outperform bonds. Correlations between policy uncertainty and Stock/Bond valuations indicate that (US) equities have downside risk.

Chart 5: Over 10Y, equity returns have aligned with GDP growth in DMs but not EMs. This, along with (IMF forecasts) of US growth moderating and high EM growth, could suggest structural tailwinds to EM equities.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of May 31, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Trade policy uncertainty: how did markets fare through the volatility?

The announcement of tariffs on April 2nd sent shock waves through financial markets. However, the original announcements were soon superseded by announcements of delays or trade talks that re-kindled optimism, leading to a bounce-back in many market segments. By the end of April, most equity markets had almost fully retraced, but there was a divergence, with the US and China trailing Europe and APAC in USD terms (Chart 1). Despite the retracement, the extreme volatility seen during April is an indication of potential risk in the market (Chart 2). While equity markets retraced quickly, currency markets did not, with most developed and emerging currencies appreciating against the USD, which did not play its traditional safe-haven role (Chart 3). These currency moves drove a surprising wedge between returns in local currencies and in USD, bringing the importance of currency hedging to the fore (Chart 4). Disentangling the impact of trade policy uncertainty on the cross-section of industry returns was complicated by several other global drivers, including a) the dramatic drop in oil prices, which pulled down the Energy industry, b) steepening of yield curves that helped extend the run of Financials, and c) the resurgence of global Tech after a weak Q1. In addition, Health Care was dragged down, perhaps by other policy uncertainty in the US (Chart 5). In fixed income, most sovereign yield curves saw a steepening during the two-month period but there was a divergence between Europe / Asia-Pacific, where yields fell, and the US (and Canada), where yields rose (Chart 6).

Chart 1: The tariff announcements led to a sell-off, followed by a rapid rebound, although China and the US were both still negative at the end of April. In May, optimism led to a continued run-up.
Chart 2: Equity volatility spiked as the sell-off was followed by a rapid rebound. As equity markets retraced after tariffs were paused, volatility quickly dropped to be even lower than before the initial announcement.
Chart 3: Most currencies strengthened against the USD during April, and those moves did not reverse, leaving the Swiss franc, Euro, British pound and Japanese yen acting as safe-haven currencies.
Chart 4: The large currency moves created an outsized difference between international equity returns in different currencies as the falling US dollar strengthened most non-US returns in USD terms.

Sovereign Yield Curves

Uncertainty-led increases in term premium and higher risk from deteriorating fiscal positions getting priced into DM sovereigns. Long-run estimates of Fed rate at 8-year high.

Both real yield and term premium (Charts 2&3) have driven 10-year sovereign yields higher in recent 3M, which stabilized near levels last seen pre-GFC. US Treasury investors now demand a higher rate of return from rising US fiscal risk, confirmed by Treasury’s downgrade (Moody’s), with 7-10-year yield climbing to 4.3%. Only Chinese yields remain on a downward trend, well below global peers and approaching yields of JGBs.

G7 yield curves continued steepening (Chart 4), particularly the 20s/2s curves, pricing in more monetary easing (for Europe and UK) & long-term fiscal strain risks (bear steepening in the long end). Ultra-long JGB yields increased due to shocks from both demand (central bank tapering & lower demand from Japanese life insurers) and supply (issuance trim). It’s worth noting that what happens to JGBs may affect US Treasuries, given that Japan stands as the largest foreign creditor of US debt.

Longer-run expectation for Fed funds rate had increased by March, reflecting planned tariff increases, although the magnitude and persistence of such effects were unknown. Higher costs pass-on to consumers may result in a more rapid inflation resurgence, slowing down the Fed’s easing pace.

Credit

Credit spreads moved with tariffs, and investors demand higher risk premia following policy uncertainties. Euro credit outperform the US and EM broadly.

US corporate bond spreads have largely been driven by tariff news and trade talk progress between the US and its major trading partners over 3M (Chart 1). Spreads surged to recent highs on heightened risk aversion, due to higher uncertainty, before falling back to pre-tariff levels. Tariff pause and trade agreements between the US and its major counterparts like China and the EU eased some tensions.

HY debt, a canary in the coal mine: HY spreads broadly widened over 3M, whereas IG spreads only did so marginally. 3M changes were less pronounced vs intra-month (Charts 4 & 5 show daily movement), as temporary trade war truce have pulled spreads back from their April 7 peaks. Spread volatility spiked, more so in HY than IG, in line with equity volatility.

Despite wider spreads, Euro corporates posted substantial 3M returns of 9.7-9.8%, boosted by a stronger EUR/USD. EM corporate returns were almost flat over 3M (0.3% in both IG and HY), only outperforming US IG (-0.4%). Euro HY (13.1%) overtook EM HY (9.1%) as the top 12M performer. Euro IG also delivered double-digit 12M returns (11.4%). (Chart 3)

Credit markets responded to tariff shock in a restrained way (compared to Covid & post-Covid inflation shocks), as liquidity conditions remain benign. Euro corporate bonds trade cheaper than US and emerging markets.

Higher duration risk-adjusted returns vs 3M ago indicate that investors became more risk-averse during geopolitical and trade policy uncertainties. Within HY, the gap between the highest, EM (2.25), and the lowest, Euro (1.86), widened over 3M.

Risk-aversion affects valuations – IG valuations show a stronger comeback than HY. Ongoing trade negotiations are likely to keep uncertainty elevated, posing risks to HY valuations.

Tariff effect proves significant within a 10-year history – a large scale of shifts in spread percentile (Chart 2) and CMDI (Chart 1 4), but still not as influential as the Covid shock in 2020 or the inflation shock during 2022-23. Region wise, Euro corporate bond valuations remain more attractive (cheaper) than peers (Chart 3). This is despite US & Emerging corporates becoming cheaper over 6M, while Euro IG has become much more expensive since November 2024 contrary to the lower valuations of its corporate peers.

In contrast to the equity and HY bond markets turbulence, US liquidity conditions and market implied default rate are little changed (Chart 5).

Equities

US underperformance in 2025 has largely been driven by valuation compression, but current P/Es and real yields suggests this trend could continue.

Equity market performance was broadly positive over 3M in USD (Chart 1), led by strength in Developed Europe ex UK and Japan. YTD, the US has underperformed other major markets as policy uncertainty and risks to its growth and inflation outlooks have caused investors to become wary of higher valuations. As of May, the correlation between valuations and subsequent 10Y returns continue to suggest significantly lower returns for the US compared to those in recent years (Chart 3).

Changes in valuation appear to have been the main driver of underperformance in US equities YTD (Chart 2). However, the historical relationship between real yields and valuations suggest that further re-rating may be necessary (Chart 4). Europe, the UK and China have benefitted from a positive re-rating in valuations.

In April, the percentage of companies with negative earnings revisions picked up as trade tensions and the resultant risk to earnings caused analysts to revise estimates. However, the number of negative earnings revisions were notably lower in May, falling back towards 2-year mean levels. Any further escalation in trade tensions could re-accelerate downward earnings revisions, which sit as another downside risk to equities.

Growth forecasts make a case for continued convergence in cross-regional valuations, but risks remain in Europe.

In the context of stretched valuations, markets outside of the US appear less risky. Notably, while valuations for the FTSE USA index still sit close to 10-year highs, valuations for other developed markets, including the UK and Europe sit slightly above their 10-yr mean. Japan remains one of the only major markets with valuations below 10-year mean levels (Chart 1).

FTSE Developed is now forecast to see higher earnings growth than FTSE USA over 2Y (Chart 2), making the case for some narrowing in cross-market valuations between the US and its peers. However, survey data in Europe suggest caution, with current levels of the IFO business climate survey implying that further earnings downgrades may be warranted (Chart 3).

The rally following the news of US-China trade discussions in May was relatively broad based, even in countries where no formal deal has been reached. This suggests that any further de-escalation is likely to encourage an "everything rally" rather than one isolated to only those directly affected markets (Chart 4). In the US, retail equity ownership, a traditionally contrarian indicator of risk, now exceeds levels seen during the dot-com bubble, pointing to stretched retail investor sentiment (Chart 5).

Emerging Markets

Policy uncertainty puts downward pressure on global growth, but EM growth expected to be 2.6x that of DM. Within equities, EM outperformed DM over 3M, but lagged fixed income (largely China effect). Dispersion within EM reflects their relative exposure to US tariffs.

Growth forecasts were downgraded globally over 3M, yet financial markets posted positive returns in US dollar terms. EM’s outperformance vs DM in 3M, in both equities and ex-China rates, is in-line with their stronger growth outlook and reduced risk perception (compressed sovereign spreads).

Within EM, countries with lower exposure to US tariff hikes (due to trade deficit with the US) benefitted on a relative basis, and so Brazil led YTD gains in both equities and fixed income (Charts 2 & 3). China’s 3M performance was more subdued than peers across equity/fixed income/currency, not surprising given its centrality in US trade policy changes.

EM 7-10-year sovereign spreads versus US Treasuries have generally narrowed since the Fed’s monetary tightening started in 2022, even turned negative for some (e.g., China, Thailand, Malaysia). The Fed being on hold, rating downgrade of US debt, higher term premium, and most EM central banks being in easing mode, may further reduce EM sovereign spreads.
